title: Java Final Fields
description: Review Java 26 final-field restrictions for deep reflection.
learningGoals: Recognize that Java 26 warns when deep reflection mutates final fields without opt-in.
Prefer constructor initialization and explicit opt-in only for serialization-style migration cases.

completionChecks.prompt: What does Java 26 do by default when deep reflection mutates a final field illegally?
choices: It silently treats the field as non-final
It issues a run-time warning as preparation for future stronger restrictions
It rewrites the field as volatile
It deletes the declaring class from the module
completionChecks.explanation: JEP 500 starts with warnings for illegal final-field mutation so code can migrate before a future release tightens the default behavior.

completionChecks.prompt: Which option enables class-path code to mutate final fields when a migration truly needs it?
completionChecks.explanation: ALL-UNNAMED applies to class-path code in the unnamed module. The feature is not a preview feature and --add-modules does not enable mutation.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens at field.set(invoice, 99) without the required final-field mutation opt-in?
choices: The mutation is treated as normal assignment with no diagnostics
The JVM logs or throws an ArrayStoreException
Java 26 reports an illegal final-field mutation according to the configured mode
The record pattern matcher handles the assignment
completionChecks.explanation: The reflective write is the problematic operation: Java 26 detects the final-field mutation and reports it according to the illegal-final-field-mutation mode.
